{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.4": 0.3, "R1.5": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.0}}

Summary: Rewrote the abstract/intro to resolve internal contradictions, sharpen the contribution, and align the empirical claims with what the rest of the text actually says.

The biggest problem in the artifact is not prose — it's that the section contradicts itself in two places, and those contradictions undermine the contribution as stated. Fixing them is what "stronger" actually requires.

Contradictions and weaknesses to fix

1. Stateless vs. stateful. Paragraph 2 says the router "is stateless across queries... with no workload memory or aggregate statistics carried across requests," and that this is what enables the closed-form derivation. Paragraph 3 then says the latency head is fed by join-selectivity histograms maintained over a sliding window of the last 10,000 queries, refreshed every 500. Those are aggregate statistics carried across requests. Either the closed-form claim is wrong, or the histograms aren't actually inputs to the per-query decision (e.g., they parameterize the model offline between refreshes). Pick one and say so.

2. The headline result is mis-attributed. Paragraph 3 attributes a 38% median latency reduction to the router. Paragraph 4 then says the advisor's tier-promotion recommendations are "the principal driver of the latency improvements reported above." If the advisor causes most of the win, the routing-layer framing in paragraph 1 is misleading and the 38% number should not be presented as a router result without decomposition. Report router-only and router+advisor numbers separately.

3. The contribution is split and unranked. The piece pitches a router ("central contribution") but the punchline is an advisor. Decide which is the thesis. If both matter, say so explicitly and order them by contribution weight.

4. Evidence gaps that should be closed or acknowledged. The 4,200-pair accuracy training set is small relative to the 18,000-query evaluation; say whether eval queries are disjoint. The accuracy floor τ is referenced but never given a value or a sensitivity analysis hook. "11% relative to a static rule-of-thumb baseline" is vague — name the baseline. "Median" latency hides tail behavior on a routing system; p95/p99 should at least be promised.

5. Minor prose. "LLM-derived embeddings" is doing no work — just "embeddings" suffices unless the LLM provenance matters downstream. "Decisively cheaper" is a hedge that reads as a fact; either quantify or drop. "Practical index-tuning tool" undersells if routing really is the contribution.

Revised version

We present LIRA (Learned Index Routing Advisor), a query-routing and tier-advisory layer for hybrid analytical systems whose workloads straddle a small in-memory column store and a large disk-resident vector index over embeddings. LIRA makes two coupled contributions: (i) a per-query cost-based router that predicts the latency of each candidate backend and dispatches to the cheaper one subject to a user-specified accuracy floor τ, and (ii) an advisor that consumes the same telemetry to recommend which embedding partitions to promote to the in-memory tier. The router is the per-query mechanism; the advisor is what changes the steady-state cost landscape the router operates over, and as we show below, most of the end-to-end latency win comes from the advisor.

Cost prediction uses two regression heads: a latency head over physical-plan features (estimated rows, selectivity of the most selective predicate, presence of a join) and an accuracy head trained on a held-out validation workload of 4,200 labeled query–answer pairs, disjoint from the evaluation set. At dispatch time, the router scores both backends and selects the lower-cost option whose predicted accuracy clears τ. The per-query decision itself is a closed-form rule (§3) over the current query's features; aggregate state — specifically, join-selectivity histograms maintained over a sliding window of the most recent 10,000 queries and refreshed every 500 queries — parameterizes the latency head between refreshes but is held fixed within a routing decision. This separation is what lets the per-query rule remain closed-form while still tracking workload drift.

Empirically, on a workload of 18,000 queries replayed against a 240 GB embedding corpus, LIRA reduces median end-to-end latency by 38% relative to always-route-to-vector and by 11% relative to a static selectivity-threshold baseline. Decomposing the gain: the router alone accounts for [X%], with the remainder attributable to advisor-driven tier promotions over the trace. The router's largest per-query gains accrue on point-lookups with highly selective predicates. Following the advisor's top-3 recommendations over a 72-hour trace reduces working-set memory pressure by 22%. We position LIRA as a practical control layer for operators of hybrid embedding-backed analytical systems, with the routing rule and the advisor jointly responsible for the reported wins.

Uncertainty disclosure: I do not know the actual router-only vs. advisor-only decomposition of the 38% figure (left as [X%]); the original artifact attributes most of the gain to the advisor but does not give a number. The "disjoint from the evaluation set" claim and the renaming of the baseline to "static selectivity-threshold" are inferences from context and should be verified against the underlying methodology before publication.
